response: The EU’s GDPR, in force since May 2018, applies to any company handling EU residents’ data and gives individuals rights such as access, erasure, portability, and requiring explicit consent, with breaches reportable within 72 hours. It can impose penalties of up to 4% of global annual revenue or 20 million euros, and major fines have been levied on Meta (€1.2 billion in 2023), Amazon (€746 million), and Google (over €200 million total). The law has inspired similar privacy rules worldwide, including California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD, and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act. While critics say GDPR creates heavy compliance burdens and inconsistent enforcement, it established data privacy as a fundamental right and a global standard.
